extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based thermal incubator**, designed as a representative use case for Digital Twin (DT) engineering. The system operates in the **thermal regulation domain**, with the primary objective of maintaining a predefined temperature inside an insulated container regardless of internal content variations. Key components include:
  - **Hardware**:
    - A **styrofoam box** serving as an insulated container to minimize thermal losses.
    - A **heatbed** (electrically powered) for active heating of the internal environment.
    - A **fan** to ensure uniform temperature distribution inside the box.
    - **Three temperature sensors**:
      - Two sensors for **internal temperature monitoring** (critical for control and feedback).
      - One sensor for **external temperature monitoring** (to account for environmental influences).
  - **Cyber Components**:
    - A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the embedded controller for real-time monitoring and actuation.
    - **Software systems** for communication, control logic, and simulation model integration.
  The system is **open-source** (hardware/software specifications available at [GitHub repository](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), making it a **reproducible and extensible platform** for DT research. Its **modularity**, **scalability**, and **environmental robustness** (e.g., handling thermal inertia of the heatbed) make it suitable for DT applications requiring **predictive control, fault detection, and adaptive optimization** under varying conditions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - **Real-time sensor data ingestion** from internal/external temperature sensors, enabling **state estimation** of the physical system.
    - **Anomaly detection** via calibrated models to identify deviations (e.g., sensor faults, thermal drift).
  - **Simulation Services**:
    - **Predictive thermal modeling** using **two calibrated simulation models** (one with **2 free parameters**, another with **4 free parameters**) to replicate and forecast the incubator’s dynamic behavior under varying conditions (e.g., thermal inertia of the heatbed).
    - **What-if analysis** to evaluate the impact of changes (e.g., environmental temperature, content load) on system performance.
  - **Optimization Services**:
    - **Automated calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters using runtime data, improving accuracy for **control and predictive tasks**.
    - **Supervisory control** to adjust heating/fan operations dynamically, ensuring **optimal temperature regulation** and **energy efficiency**.
  - **Visualization Services**:
    - **Advanced visualizations** of system states (e.g., temperature gradients, control signals) for **diagnostic and decision-support purposes**.
  - **Reconfigurability Services**:
    - **Adaptive robustness** to environmental changes (e.g., external temperature fluctuations) via **runtime model updates** and **closed-loop control**.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers** for system analysis, fault diagnosis, and optimization.
    - **Operators** for real-time monitoring and intervention.
    - **Researchers** for validating DT techniques (e.g., calibration methods, model@runtime approaches).

extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study operates with a temporal granularity defined by the control loop of the Raspberry Pi-based controller. The system periodically reads temperature data from the two internal sensors and one external sensor (Section 2.1), with the control loop parameters (e.g., heating duration and waiting duration) explicitly configured in Section 2.2.3. The calibration process for the thermal models (Model A with two parameters and Model B with four parameters) involves experimental data collection, but no specific synchronization frequency or latency requirements are documented. The only explicit temporal aspect is the periodic monitoring of temperature data by the low-level driver, which is relayed to the controller for real-time adjustments. No real-time visualization or Kalman filter-based anomaly detection is explicitly implemented or detailed in the provided documents, as these are referenced only as future goals in Section 5. The time scale for simulations (e.g., what-if scenarios) is not quantified in the provided text, but the system is designed to support co-simulations using historical or real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system for the incubator is implemented as a **single, centralized instance** aligned with the CPS-based Digital Twin architecture depicted in Figure 1. It integrates three core components: **Data** (collected from the PT via sensors), **Models** (two calibrated models, Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters, where Model B was empirically determined to better predict thermal inertia), and **Algorithms** (e.g., calibration algorithms, Kalman filter for state estimation, and supervisory controllers for decision-making). The system does not exhibit decentralized multiplicities or hierarchical structures; instead, it relies on a **closed-loop architecture** where the DT monitors the PT, detects anomalies, and triggers recalibration or controller adjustments. The **self-adaptation loop** (Section 5) is envisioned as a **multi-step process** (e.g., anomaly detection, data gathering, model recalibration, and controller reconfiguration) but is not yet fully implemented in the provided documents. The DT does not dynamically select models autonomously; instead, researchers manually identified Model B as superior for further development.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator is structured around a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) architecture**, integrating the following key components:
  1. **Physical Twin (PT)**: Comprising an insulated styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and three temperature sensors (two internal, one external) (Section 2.1).
  2. **Cyber Twin (CT)**: Implemented on a Raspberry Pi, featuring:
     - A **low-level driver** for sensor data acquisition and actuator control (Section 2.2.2).
     - A **RabbitMQ communication server** for message relay between the driver and controller (Section 2.2.1).
     - A **temperature controller** (PID or similar) to regulate heating based on sensor feedback (Section 2.2.3).
  3. **Simulation Models**: Two calibrated thermal models (Model A and Model B) used for predictive analysis, with Model B preferred for its accuracy in thermal inertia modeling (Section 3).
  The system does not explicitly detail resource management or system-wide coordination mechanisms beyond the controller-driver-communication loop. The proposed self-adaptation loop (Section 5) is not implemented but would involve dynamic recalibration of Model B, Kalman filter updates, and controller reconfiguration—none of which are described in the provided documents. The constellation lacks explicit mention of orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven) or integration with external orchestration frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study integrates **two distinct simulation models** designed to predict the internal temperature dynamics of the physical twin (PT). The first model employs **two free parameters**, while the second, more accurate model uses **four free parameters**, enabling better prediction of the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**. Both models are **calibrated** using real-time data from the PT to refine parameter estimates, ensuring alignment with observed behavior. The DT framework leverages **data collected from sensors** (e.g., temperature sensors inside and outside the insulated container) to update model parameters dynamically via **calibration algorithms**. These models are **behavioral** (focused on thermal dynamics) and **analytical** (mathematically described, likely via differential equations or system identification techniques). The DT architecture also includes **algorithms** for monitoring (e.g., fault detection, supervisory control) and optimization, which manipulate data and models to generate actionable insights. Data management involves real-time ingestion from the PT, storage for historical analysis, and bidirectional communication between the cyber (Raspberry Pi-based controller) and physical components. The models are **hierarchical** in abstraction, with trade-offs between accuracy (higher-fidelity models) and computational efficiency (simpler models).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The incubator’s physical acting components include:
  1. **Heatbed**: A resistive heating element embedded in the insulated container, controlled via a **Raspberry Pi-based controller** to regulate internal temperature. The control mechanism operates as a **bang-bang controller**, cycling the heatbed on/off to maintain setpoint stability. No explicit duty-cycle modulation (e.g., PWM) is documented; activation is binary (on/off) based on sensor feedback.
  2. **Fan**: A low-power fan distributed within the styrofoam box to ensure **uniform thermal distribution** of heat. The fan’s operation is tied to the heatbed’s activation, though no independent control logic is specified.
  3. **Controller (Raspberry Pi)**: Acts as the central **cyber-physical interface**, executing control algorithms and interfacing with actuators/sensors. The system incorporates a **safe protocol** in the low-level driver to prevent unsafe conditions (e.g., overheating), though no explicit emergency shutdown thresholds or unique actuator identifiers (e.g., 64-bit serial codes) are detailed for the heatbed or fan.
  Actuation ranges are constrained by the **thermal capacity of the heatbed** and the **insulation properties of the styrofoam box**, with no documented limits on maximum power or temperature. Communication between the controller and actuators uses **generic GPIO interfaces**, not specialized protocols like CAN or Modbus.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The sensing infrastructure comprises:
  - **Three 1-Wire Digital Thermometers** (DS18B20 or equivalent) connected via a **1-wire bus**:
    1. **Two internal sensors**: Monitor the temperature inside the styrofoam box with an **accuracy of ±0.5°C**. Their spatial distribution is unspecified but implies coverage of critical zones (e.g., near the heatbed and opposite side).
    2. **One external sensor**: Measures ambient temperature outside the box to account for environmental influences on the PT’s thermal behavior.
  - **Sampling frequency**: Not explicitly quantified in the documents, but implied to be **low-latency** (e.g., sub-second) to support real-time control and model calibration. No precision specifications (±0.1°C) or transmission rates (e.g., I2C/SPI) are provided beyond the **1-wire bus** protocol. Data collection is managed by the **Raspberry Pi controller**, which aggregates sensor readings for model input and monitoring purposes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
Model fidelity is evaluated through **experimental validation**, where the **four-parameter model** outperformed the two-parameter model in predicting the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, a critical factor for accurate temperature regulation. **Calibration** is performed using a **non-linear least squares solver** to minimize the discrepancy between simulated and measured temperatures, ensuring parameter estimates align with real-world behavior. Validation methods include:
  - **Comparative experiments**: Direct testing of both models against real-time sensor data to assess predictive accuracy.
  - **Parameter sensitivity analysis**: Implicit in the calibration process, though not explicitly detailed.
  Uncertainty sources are not quantified (e.g., no standard deviation or confidence intervals are provided), nor are specific **error detection/correction mechanisms** (e.g., isolation forests or machine learning classifiers) documented. The DT framework relies on **fault detectors** as a general capability (per Document 1), but no system-specific implementations (e.g., threshold-based alerts or adaptive recalibration) are described. Quality assurance is ensured through **iterative model refinement** and the use of **real-time sensor feedback**, though no formal verification protocols (e.g., model-in-the-loop testing) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the incubator Digital Twin (DT) system involves the transmission of **temperature sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) to the Digital Twin. The data flow includes:
- **Data Types Transmitted**: Raw temperature readings from **three sensors** (two inside the insulated styrofoam box and one outside) with **analog/digital conversion** handled by the low-level driver.
- **Communication Protocol**: Data is transmitted via **RabbitMQ**, an open-source message broker, using **message-based communication** for synchronization and data exchange.
- **Transmission Frequencies**: The low-level driver **periodically reads temperature data** (exact frequency not specified, but implied to be real-time or near-real-time for control purposes).
- **Event Triggers**: Data transmission is triggered by the **low-level driver** polling the sensors and forwarding readings to the RabbitMQ server.
- **Data Quality Assurance**: Implicitly handled by the **safe protocol** embedded in the low-level driver to ensure data integrity and system safety.
- **Preprocessing Steps**: The low-level driver **validates and formats** the sensor data before uploading it to the RabbitMQ server for further processing by the DT.
- **Data Integration Mechanisms**: The DT components **listen to messages** published by the low-level driver via RabbitMQ, enabling seamless integration of sensor data into the DT's models and algorithms for calibration, monitoring, and simulation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT is governed by a **bang-bang controller** deployed on the Raspberry Pi, which issues **binary control commands** to the PT’s actuators (heatbed and fan). The controller receives **temperature measurements** from the RabbitMQ server and executes a **state-based control logic** defined by four parameters: **LL (Lower Limit)**, **UL (Upper Limit)**, **H (Heating Duration)**, and **C (Waiting Duration)**. When the measured temperature falls below **LL**, the controller activates the **heatbed** for **H seconds**, then waits for **C seconds** to avoid overshoot. Conversely, if the temperature exceeds **UL**, the controller deactivates the heatbed. The **low-level driver** translates these commands into **actuator signals** (e.g., PWM for the heatbed) and enforces a **safe protocol** to prevent unsafe states (e.g., overheating). No explicit **command validation/authentication** (e.g., digital signatures, checksums) or **conflict resolution** mechanisms (e.g., priority arbitration) are documented. However, the **statechart-based controller** inherently ensures deterministic behavior, and the **RabbitMQ server** acts as a intermediary for command routing. The **feedback system** relies on the closed-loop nature of the controller, where sensor data continuously informs control decisions.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . **Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative

thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further

develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
